intro: Levi (meaning joined; associated), the third son of Jacob and Leah, was named by his mother in hope that her husband would now be "joined" to her (Gen. 29:34). With his brother Simeon he avenged the violation of Dinah by slaughtering the men of Shechem (Gen. 34:25–26), drawing Jacob's curse that their descendants would be scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:5–7). Yet this curse was transformed into blessing: the Levites' zealous stand for the LORD in the golden calf incident (Ex. 32:26–29) consecrated them to priestly service, and their "scattering" through all the tribes became the instrument by which they taught the law across Israel. The tribe received no territorial inheritance; the LORD himself was their portion (Num. 18:20; Deut. 18:2). In the New Testament, Levi is also the name of the tax collector who became the apostle Matthew (Mark 2:14) and of two ancestors in Jesus' genealogy (Luke 3:24, 29).
